children.name: Manis
children.description: Asian pangolins — four species found across South and Southeast Asia. Pangolins are the only mammals with true scales, made of keratin (the same protein as human fingernails and rhino horns). When threatened, they roll into a tight ball, with the overlapping scales creating near-impenetrable armour — lions and leopards have been recorded failing to unroll pangolins. Pangolins feed exclusively on ants and termites, which they excavate with powerful claws and collect with a tongue longer than their body (which anchors to their pelvis). They have no teeth; their muscular stomach grinds insects with ingested sand and small stones. Asian pangolins are classified as critically endangered — they are the world's most trafficked wild mammals, with an estimated one million taken from the wild in the 2000s for use in traditional medicine and as luxury food.

children.name: Manis javanica
children.description: Critically endangered. The most heavily trafficked wild mammal species. Found in forests from Myanmar to Indonesia. Nocturnal and arboreal; uses a prehensile tail to climb trees. Populations have crashed due to poaching for scales and meat.

children.name: Manis pentadactyla
children.description: Critically endangered. Historically distributed across much of South and East Asia; now functionally extinct across most of its range due to poaching. Distinguished by more scales and a broader head than the Sunda pangolin.

children.name: Manis crassicaudata
children.description: Endangered. Found across the Indian subcontinent from Pakistan to Sri Lanka. The largest of the Asian pangolins. Can close its nostrils and ear openings when feeding to prevent ant bites.

children.name: Manis culionensis
children.description: Endemic to the Palawan Island group of the Philippines, the Philippine Pangolin was long treated as a subspecies of the Sunda Pangolin before genetic and morphological analysis confirmed its species status. Critically endangered, it faces intense poaching pressure for export to Chinese markets alongside severe deforestation across its small island range. It inhabits primary and secondary tropical forests, sheltering in burrows by day and foraging for ants and termites at night.

children.name: Smutsia
children.description: African ground pangolins — two species of large, terrestrial pangolins found in sub-Saharan Africa. Temminck's ground pangolin is the most widespread African species, found across the continent south of the Sahara. The giant ground pangolin is the world's largest pangolin, reaching 1.4 m in length. Ground pangolins are primarily terrestrial, walking on their hind legs with their forelegs held away from the body. African pangolins are better studied than Asian species but remain threatened by local hunting and, increasingly, international trafficking to Asia.

children.name: Smutsia temminckii
children.description: The most widespread African pangolin, from Ethiopia to South Africa. Vulnerable. Digs for termites in savannah and open woodland. Mothers carry single young on their tails. Active mainly at night; relies on smell to locate ant colonies.

children.name: Smutsia gigantea
children.description: The world's largest pangolin at up to 1.4 m. Vulnerable. Associated with moist forest and gallery forest along rivers. Can consume over 200,000 ants in a single night. Poorly known due to its elusive habits.

children.name: Phataginus
children.description: African tree pangolins — two small, arboreal species found in the rainforests of central and west Africa. Tree pangolins have longer tails relative to body length, with bare scales on the underside of the tail tip that allow gripping branches. The white-bellied pangolin is the most common African species and the most frequently encountered in markets. Tree pangolins are almost entirely nocturnal and arboreal, sleeping in tree hollows by day. Like all pangolins, they are solitary except when mating and when females are caring for young.

children.name: Phataginus tricuspis
children.description: The most frequently traded African pangolin. Vulnerable. A skilled climber in the forest canopy of central Africa. Scales have three-cusped outer edges, giving the species its scientific name.

children.name: Phataginus tetradactyla
children.description: The smallest African pangolin and the one with the most proportionally elongated tail, which is used as a prehensile fifth limb for navigating the forest canopy of West and Central Africa. Unlike most pangolins, it is frequently active during the day, foraging for arboreal ant colonies. Listed as Vulnerable by the IUCN, it is threatened by habitat loss and targeted hunting in parts of its range.
